en.extract:
The snowy albatross (Diomedea exulans), also known as the wandering albatross, white-winged albatross, or goonie, is a large seabird from the family Diomedeidae; they have a circumpolar range in the Southern Ocean. It is the largest species of albatross and was long considered to be the same species as the Tristan albatross and the Antipodean albatross. Together with the Amsterdam albatross, it forms the wandering albatross species complex, which some began referring to more recently as "snowy".
The snowy albatross is one of the two largest members of the genus Diomedea (the great albatrosses), being similar in size to the southern royal albatross. It has the greatest known wingspan of any living bird and is also one of the most far-ranging birds. Some individual snowy albatrosses are known to circumnavigate the Southern Ocean three times in one year, covering more than 120,000 km (75,000 mi).


Taxonomy
The taxonomic history of this species is long and complicated. The species was first described as Diomedea exulans by Carl Linnaeus in 1758, based on a specimen from the Cape of Good Hope. Diomedea refers to Diomedes from Greek mythology whose companions turned to birds, and exulans or exsul are Latin for "exile" or "wanderer" referring to its extensive flights. The type locality has been restricted to South Georgia.
Some experts considered there to be four subspecies of D. exulans, which they elevated to species status, and use the term wandering albatross to refer to a species complex that includes the proposed species D. antipodensis, D. dabbenena, D. exulans, and D. gibsoni.


Description
The plumage varies with age, with the juveniles starting chocolate brown. As they age they become whiter. The adults have white bodies with white and black wings. Males have whiter wings than females, with just the tips and trailing edges of the wings black. The snowy albatross is the whitest of the wandering albatross species complex, the other species having a great deal more brown and black on the wings and body, very closely resembling immature wandering albatrosses. The large bill is pink, as are the feet. They also have a salt gland that is situated above the nasal passage and helps desalinate their bodies, due to the high amount of ocean water that they imbibe. They excrete a high saline solution from their nose, which is a probable cause for the pink-yellow stain seen on some animals' necks.


Size

The snowy albatross has the longest wingspan of any living bird, reaching up to 3.7 m (12 ft), with a mean span of 3.1 m (10 ft 2 in) in Bird Island, South Georgia. Wingspan measured an average of 3 m (9 ft 10 in) in 123 birds measured off the coast of Malabar, New South Wales. On the Crozet Islands, adults averaged 3.05 m (10 ft 0 in) in wingspan. The longest-winged specimens have been about 3.7 m (12 ft 2 in). Two specimens have been reported having wingspans of 4.22 m (13 ft 10 in) and 5.3 m (17 ft 5 in), but these reports remain unverified. As a result of its large wingspan, it is capable of remaining in the air without flapping its wings for several hours at a time (traveling 22m for every meter of drop). The length of the body is about 107 to 135 cm (3 ft 6 in to 4 ft 5 in) with females being slightly smaller than males.
Adults can weigh from 5.9 to 12.7 kg (13 to 28 lb), although most will weigh 6.35 to 11.91 kg (14.0 to 26.3 lb). On Macquarie Island, three males averaged 8.4 kg (19 lb) and three females averaged 6.2 kg (14 lb). In parts of the Crozet Islands, males averaged 9.44 kg (20.8 lb) while females averaged 7.84 kg (17.3 lb). However, 10 unsexed adults from the Crozet Islands averaged 9.6 kg (21 lb). On South Georgia, 52 males were found to average 9.11 kg (20.1 lb) while 53 females were found to average 7.27 kg (16.0 lb). On Île de la Possession, adult male snowy albatrosses averaged 10.92 kg (24.1 lb) while adult females averaged 8.87 kg (19.6 lb). Another sampling of adult body masses from the same colony found males to average 11.09 kg (24.4 lb) and females to average 9.1 kg (20 lb). Immature birds have been recorded weighing as much as 16.1 kg (35 lb) during their first flights (at which time they may still have fat reserves that will be shed as they continue to fly). On South Georgia, fledglings were found to average 10.9 kg (24 lb). Albatrosses from outside the "snowy" wandering albatross group (D. exulans) are smaller but are now generally deemed to belong to different species.


Distribution and habitat
The snowy albatross breeds on South Georgia Island, Crozet Islands, Kerguelen Islands, Prince Edward Islands, and Macquarie Island, is seen feeding year-round off the Kaikōura Peninsula on the east coast of the South Island of New Zealand, and ranges in all the southern oceans from 28° to 60°. Some individual snowy albatrosses are known to circumnavigate the Southern Ocean three times, covering more than 120,000 km (75,000 mi), in one year. Snowy albatrosses spend most of their life in flight, landing only to breed and feed. Distances traveled each year are difficult to measure, but one banded bird was recorded traveling 6,000 km (3,700 mi) in twelve days.


Behavior


Feeding

Snowy albatrosses travel vast distances to find food, and tend to feed further out in open oceans and in colder waters further south, whereas other albatross such as the related royal albatross tend to forage in somewhat shallower waters and closer to continental shelves. They feed at the surface, mainly during daylight hours, and are not well adapted for diving. During flight, speeds of 135 km/h (84 mph) have been recorded. They feed on cephalopods, small fish, and crustaceans. The species has been shown to be attracted to fishing vessels, foraging on bait, caught fish, and offal.


Reproduction

The snowy albatross mates for life and breeds every other year. At breeding time they occupy loose colonies on isolated island groups in the Southern Ocean. When courting they will spread their wings, wave their heads, and rap their bills together while braying. Wanderers have a large range of displays from screams and whistles to grunts and bill clapping. They lay one egg that is white, with a few spots, and is about 10 cm (3.9 in) long. They lay between 10 December and 5 January. The nests are large bowls built of grassy vegetation and soil peat, that is 1 meter wide at the base and half a meter wide at the apex. Incubation takes about 11 weeks and both parents are involved. The 11-week incubation period is among the longest of any bird. During the early stages of the chick's development, the parents take turns sitting on the nest while the other searches for food. Later, both adults search for food and visit the chick at irregular intervals. Researchers previously assumed that chicks went without food for the whole winter after a weaning period of roughly 12 to 16 weeks. Later studies disproved this concept, as chicks were found to be feeding during this period. They are a monogamous species, usually for life. Adolescents return to the colony within six years; however, they will not start breeding until 11 to 15 years. About 31.5% of fledglings survive. They can live for over 50 years.


Relationship with humans

Sailors used to capture the birds for their long wing bones, from which they made tobacco pipe stems.  The early explorers of the great Southern Sea were cheered by the companionship of the albatross in their dreary solitudes, and as shown in Coleridge's The Rime of the Ancient Mariner killing them was seen as extremely bad luck. The metaphor of "an albatross around his neck" also comes from the poem and indicates an unwanted burden causing anxiety or hindrance. In the days of sail the bird often accompanied ships for days, not merely following a ship, but wheeling in wide circles around it without ever being observed to land on the water. The bird would continue its flight, apparently untired, in tempestuous as well as moderate weather.
The Māori of New Zealand used albatrosses as a food source. They caught them using baited hooks. Because the wing bones of albatross are light but very strong, Māori used them to make a number of different items including kōauau (flutes), needles, tattooing chisel blades, and barbs for fish hooks.


Conservation

The IUCN lists the snowy albatross as vulnerable status. Adult mortality is 5% to 7.8% per year as of 2003. It has an occurrence range of 64,700,000 km2 (25,000,000 sq mi), although its breeding range is only 1,900 km2 (730 sq mi).
The biggest threat to their survival is that they are attracted by the bait for longline fishing and get caught in the same way as the fish; however, pollution is also taking a toll, mainly from plastics and abandoned fishing hooks. The CCAMLR has introduced measures to reduce bycatch of albatrosses around South Georgia by 99%, and other regional fishing commissions are taking similar measures to reduce fatalities. The Prince Edward Islands are a nature preserve, the Macquarie Islands are a World Heritage site, and large parts of the Crozet Islands and the Kerguelen Islands are nature reserves.


References


Further reading
Lindsey, Terence (1986). The Seabirds of Australia. Angus & Robertson. ISBN 978-0-207-15192-7.
Marchant, Stephen; Higgins, Peter Jeffrey (1990). Handbook of Australian, New Zealand and Antarctic Birds: Ratites to ducks. Vol. 1. Oxford University Press. ISBN 978-0-19-553068-1.
Parmelee, David Freeland (1980). Bird Island in Antarctic Waters. University of Minnesota Press. ISBN 978-0-8166-0937-6.
Lindsey, Terence (22 June 2008). Albatrosses. Csiro Publishing. ISBN 978-0-643-09852-7.


External links

Species factsheet – BirdLife International
Fact file – ARKive
Video, photos and sounds – Internet Bird Collection
Holotype photos – Collections Online, Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa
Do albatrosses have personalities? – YouTube video, Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa

The '''snowy albatross''' ('''''Diomedea exulans'''''), also known as the '''wandering albatross''', '''white-winged albatross''', or '''goonie''', is a large [[seabird]] from the [[family (biology)|family]] [[Diomedeidae]]; they have a circumpolar range in the [[Southern Ocean]]. It is the largest [[species]] of [[albatross]] and was long considered to be the same species as the [[Tristan albatross]] and the [[Antipodean albatross]]. Together with the [[Amsterdam albatross]], it forms the wandering albatross [[Cryptic species complex|species complex]], which some began referring to more recently as "snowy".<ref>https://acap.aq/latest-news/the-acap-monthly-missive-is-there-or-should-there-be-such-a-thing-as-a-snowy-albatross</ref>

The snowy albatross is one of the two largest members of the [[genus]] ''Diomedea'' (the [[great albatross]]es), being similar in size to the [[southern royal albatross]]. It has the greatest known wingspan of any living bird and is also one of the most far-ranging birds. Some individual snowy albatrosses are known to [[circumnavigation|circumnavigate]] the [[Southern Ocean]] three times in one year, covering more than {{cvt|120,000|km|mi}}.

==Taxonomy==
The taxonomic history of this species is long and complicated.<ref>{{cite journal | url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/j.1365-294X.2004.02232.x | doi=10.1111/j.1365-294X.2004.02232.x | title=Global population structure and taxonomy of the wandering albatross species complex | date=2004 | last1=Burg | first1=T. M. | last2=Croxall | first2=J. P. | journal=Molecular Ecology | volume=13 | issue=8 | pages=2345–2355 | pmid=15245406 | bibcode=2004MolEc..13.2345B | url-access=subscription }}</ref> The species was [[Species description|first described]] as ''Diomedea exulans'' by [[Carl Linnaeus]] in [[10th edition of Systema Naturae|1758]], based on a specimen from the [[Cape of Good Hope]].<ref name="Robertsonft" /> ''Diomedea'' refers to [[Diomedes]] from [[Greek mythology]] whose companions turned to birds, and ''exulans'' or {{Lang|la|exsul}} are [[latin language|Latin]] for "exile" or "wanderer" referring to its extensive flights.<ref name=Gotch/> The type locality has been restricted to South Georgia.<ref>{{Cite journal |doi = 10.11646/zootaxa.4236.1.7|title = Settling the name ''Diomedea exulans'' Linnaeus, 1758 for the Wandering Albatross by neotypification|year = 2017|last1 = Schodde|first1 = Richard|last2 = Tennyson|first2 = Alan J.D.|last3 = Groth|first3 = Jeff G.|last4 = Lai|first4 = Jonas|last5 = Scofield|first5 = Paul|last6 = Steinheimer|first6 = Frank D.|journal = Zootaxa|volume = 4236|issue = 1|page = 135|pmid = 28264342}}</ref>

Some experts considered there to be four [[subspecies]] of ''D. exulans'', which they elevated to species status, and use the term ''wandering albatross'' to refer to a [[species complex]] that includes the proposed species ''[[Antipodean albatross|D. antipodensis]]'', ''[[Tristan albatross|D. dabbenena]]'', ''D. exulans'', and ''D. gibsoni''.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Burg|first1=T. M.|last2=Croxall|first2=J. P.|title=Global population structure and taxonomy of the wandering albatross species complex|journal=Molecular Ecology|volume=13|issue=8|year=2004|pages=2345–2355|doi=10.1111/j.1365-294X.2004.02232.x|pmid=15245406 |bibcode=2004MolEc..13.2345B |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1111/j.1365-294X.2004.02232.x|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

==Description==

The [[plumage]] varies with age, with the juveniles starting chocolate brown. As they age they become whiter.<ref name="Robertsonft" /> The adults have white bodies with white and black wings. Males have whiter wings than females, with just the tips and trailing edges of the wings black. The snowy albatross is the whitest of the wandering albatross species complex, the other species having a great deal more brown and black on the wings and body, very closely resembling immature wandering albatrosses. The large bill is pink, as are the feet.<ref name="BLI" /> They also have a [[salt gland]] that is situated above the nasal passage and helps desalinate their bodies, due to the high amount of ocean water that they imbibe. They excrete a high saline solution from their nose, which is a probable cause for the pink-yellow stain seen on some animals' necks.<ref name="Ehrlich" /><ref>{{cite journal|author=Tickell, W.L.N. |year=2011|title=Plumage contamination on Wandering Albatrosses – an aerodynamic model|journal=Sea Swallow |volume=60|pages= 67–69}}</ref>

=== Size ===
[[File:Diomedea exulans in flight 2 - SE Tasmania.jpg|thumb|upright|In flight]]
The snowy albatross has the longest [[wingspan]] of any living bird, reaching up to {{convert|3.7|m||||abbr=on}},<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sanchez |first1=Gerardo |last2=Salazar |first2=Ryan D. |last3=Hassanalian |first3=Mostafa |last4=Abdelkefi |first4=Abdessattar |date=8 January 2018 |title=Sizing and performance analysis of albatross-inspired tilt-wing unmanned air vehicle |url=https://arc.aiaa.org/doi/10.2514/6.2018-1445 |journal=Dynamic Loads, Response, and Stability of Aerospace Vehicles |series=AIAA SciTech Forum |language=en |publisher=American Institute of Aeronautics and Astronautics |doi=10.2514/6.2018-1445 |isbn=978-1-62410-532-6|url-access=subscription }}</ref><ref name=":0">{{Cite book |last=Barwell |first=Graham |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=I5AOBQAAQBAJ&q=Albatross+size |title=Albatross |date=15 October 2013 |publisher=Reaktion Books |isbn=978-1-78023-214-0 |pages=12–14 |language=en}}</ref> with a mean span of {{convert|3.1|m|ftin|abbr=on}} in [[Bird Island, South Georgia]]. Wingspan measured an average of {{convert|3|m|ftin|abbr=on}} in 123 birds measured off the coast of [[Malabar, New South Wales]].<ref name="Robertsonft" /><ref name="Dunn"/><ref name = "Wood">{{cite book | author = Wood, Gerald | url = https://archive.org/details/guinnessbookofan00wood | title = The Guinness Book of Animal Facts and Feats | year = 1983 | publisher = Guinness Superlatives | isbn = 978-0-85112-235-9 }}</ref> On the [[Crozet Islands]], adults averaged {{convert|3.05|m|ftin|abbr=on}} in wingspan.<ref name= "Shaffer">{{cite journal | last1 = Shaffer | first1 = S. A. | last2 = Weimerskirch | first2 = H. | last3 = Costa | first3 = D. P. | year = 2001 | title = Functional significance of sexual dimorphism in Wandering Albatrosses, Diomedea exulans | journal = Functional Ecology | volume = 15 | issue = 2| pages = 203–210 | doi=10.1046/j.1365-2435.2001.00514.x| s2cid = 49358426 | doi-access = free | bibcode = 2001FuEco..15..203S }}</ref> The longest-winged specimens have been about {{convert|3.7|m|ftin|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Wood"/> Two specimens have been reported having wingspans of {{convert|4.22|m|ftin|abbr=on}} and {{convert|5.3|m|ftin|abbr=on}}, but these reports remain unverified.<ref name = "Wood"/> As a result of its large wingspan, it is capable of remaining in the air without flapping its wings for several hours at a time (traveling 22m for every meter of drop).<ref name=Rattenborg/> The length of the body is about {{convert|107|to|135|cm|ftin|abbr=on}}<ref name="Dunn" /> with females being slightly smaller than males.<ref name="Harrison"/><ref name="BLI" />

Adults can weigh from {{convert|5.9|to|12.7|kg|lb|abbr=on}}, although most will weigh {{convert|6.35|to|11.91|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Robertsonft" /><ref name = "Wood"/><ref>[http://www.arkive.org/wandering-albatross/diomedea-exulans Wandering albatross (''Diomedea exulans'')]. arkive.org {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110215054156/http://www.arkive.org/wandering-albatross/diomedea-exulans/ |date=15 February 2011 }}</ref><ref>"Encyclopedia of the Antarctic, v. 1 & v. 2. Beau Riffenburgh (Editor). Routledge, {{ISBN|978-0-415-97024-2}}.</ref> On [[Macquarie Island]], three males averaged {{convert|8.4|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and three females averaged {{convert|6.2|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name = "CRC">''CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses'' by John B. Dunning Jr. (Editor). CRC Press (1992), {{ISBN|978-0-8493-4258-5}}.</ref> In parts of the [[Crozet Islands]], males averaged {{convert|9.44|kg|lb|abbr=on}} while females averaged {{convert|7.84|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name="Shaffer"/> However, 10 unsexed adults from the Crozet Islands averaged {{convert|9.6|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Weimerskirch | first1 = H. | last2 = Shaffer | first2 = S. Dunning Jr. (Editor). CRC Press (2008), {{ISBN|978-1-4200-6444-5}}.</ref> On [[Île de la Possession]], adult male snowy albatrosses averaged {{convert|10.92|kg|lb|abbr=on}} while adult females averaged {{convert|8.87|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref> Shaffer, S. A., Costa, D. P., & Weimerskirch, H. (2003). ''Foraging effort in relation to the constraints of reproduction in free-ranging albatrosses''. Functional Ecology, 66–74.</ref> Another sampling of adult body masses from the same colony found males to average {{convert|11.09|kg|lb|abbr=on}} and females to average {{convert|9.1|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref> Weimerskirch, H., Cherel, Y., Cuenot-Chaillet, F., & Ridoux, V. (1997). ''Alternative foraging strategies and resource allocation by male and female wandering albatrosses''. Ecology, 78(7), 2051–2063. </ref> Immature birds have been recorded weighing as much as {{convert|16.1|kg|lb|abbr=on}} during their first flights (at which time they may still have fat reserves that will be shed as they continue to fly).<ref name = "Wood"/> On South Georgia, fledglings were found to average {{convert|10.9|kg|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1007/s00227-002-0962-y|title=Inter-annual variation in the cephalopod component of the diet of the wandering albatross, ''Diomedea exulans'', breeding at Bird Island, South Georgia|year=2003|last1=Xavier|first1=J.|last2=Croxall|first2=J.|last3=Trathan|first3=P.|last4=Rodhouse|first4=P.|journal=Marine Biology|volume=142|issue=3|pages=611–622|bibcode=2003MarBi.142..611X |s2cid=83466498}}</ref> Albatrosses from outside the "snowy" wandering albatross group (''D. exulans'') are smaller but are now generally deemed to belong to different species.<ref name= "CRC2"/><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Burg | first1 = T. M. | last2 = Croxall | first2 = J. P. | year = 2004 | title = Global population structure and taxonomy of the wandering albatross species complex | journal = Molecular Ecology | volume = 13 | issue = 8| pages = 2345–2355 | doi=10.1111/j.1365-294x.2004.02232.x | pmid=15245406| bibcode = 2004MolEc..13.2345B | s2cid = 28479284 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The snowy albatross breeds on [[South Georgia Island]], [[Crozet Islands]], [[Kerguelen Islands]], [[Prince Edward Islands]], and [[Macquarie Island]], is seen feeding year-round off the [[Kaikōura]] Peninsula on the east coast of the [[South Island]] of [[New Zealand]],<ref name=Shirihai/> and ranges in all the southern oceans from 28° to 60°.<ref name="iucn status 19 November 2021" /> Some individual snowy albatrosses are known to [[circumnavigation|circumnavigate]] the [[Southern Ocean]] three times, covering more than {{cvt|120,000|km|mi}}, in one year.<ref>{{cite journal|doi=10.1038/srep08853|title=Extreme variation in migration strategies between and within snowy albatross populations during their sabbatical year and their fitness consequences|year=2015|last1=Weimerskirch|first1=Henri|last2=Delord|first2=Karine|last3=Guitteaud|first3=Audrey|last4=Phillips|first4=Richard A.|last5=Pinet|first5=Patrick|journal=Scientific Reports|volume=5|page=8853|pmid=25747757|pmc=4352845|bibcode=2015NatSR...5.8853W}}</ref> Snowy albatrosses spend most of their life in flight, landing only to breed and feed. Distances traveled each year are difficult to measure, but one banded bird was recorded traveling {{Convert|6000|km|abbr=on}} in twelve days.<ref name="BLI"/><ref name=Richardson>Richardson, Michael (27 Sep 2002). "[http://www.iht.com/articles/2002/09/27/albatross_ed3_.php Endangered seabirds / New fishing techniques undercut an old talisman : Modern mariners pose rising threat to the albatross.] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20090221181345/http://www.iht.com/articles/2002/09/27/albatross_ed3_.php |date=2009-02-21 }}" ''Herald Tribune''.</ref>

==Behavior==

=== Feeding ===

[[File:Diomedea exulans in flight - SE Tasmania.jpg|thumb|Snowy albatrosses have the longest wingspan of any living bird.]]

Snowy albatrosses travel vast distances to find food,<ref name="WeimerskirchWilson92">{{Cite journal |last1=Weimerskirch |first1=H |last2=Wilson |first2=RP |date=1992 |title=When do wandering albatrosses Diomedea exulans forage? |journal=Marine Ecology Progress Series |volume=86 |pages=297–300 |doi=10.3354/meps086297 |bibcode=1992MEPS...86..297W |issn=0171-8630}}</ref> and tend to feed further out in open oceans and in colder waters further south, whereas other albatross such as the related royal albatross tend to forage in somewhat shallower waters and closer to continental shelves.<ref>{{cite journal|title=Diet and Feeding Ecology of the Royal Albatross ''Diomedea epomophora'' – King of the Shelf Break and Inner Slope|year=1999|doi=10.1071/MU99023|last1=Imber|first1=M.J.|journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology|volume=99|issue=3|pages=200–211|bibcode=1999EmuAO..99..200I }}</ref> They feed at the surface, mainly during daylight hours, and are not well adapted for diving.<ref name="WeimerskirchWilson92" /> During flight, speeds of {{Convert|135|km/h|abbr=unit}} have been recorded.<ref name=":0" /> They feed on [[cephalopods]], small fish, and [[crustaceans]].<ref name="Robertsonft" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Croxall |first1=J. P. |last2=North |first2=A. W. |last3=Prince |first3=P. A. |date=1 October 1988 |title=Fish prey of the Wandering Albatross Diomedea exulans at South Georgia |journal=Polar Biology |language=en |volume=9 |issue=1 |pages=9–16 |doi=10.1007/BF00441760 |bibcode=1988PoBio...9....9C |s2cid=27754838 |issn=1432-2056}}</ref> The species has been shown to be attracted to fishing vessels, foraging on bait, caught fish, and offal.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Cherel|first1=Y.|last2=Weimerskirch|first2=H.|last3=Duhamel|first3=G.|year=1996|title=Interactions between longline vessels and seabirds in Kerguelen water and a method to reduce seabird mortality|journal=Biological Conservation|volume=75|issue=1|pages=63–70|doi=10.1016/0006-3207(95)00037-2|bibcode=1996BCons..75...63C }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Collet|first1=J.|last2=Patrick|first2=S.C.|last3=Weimerskirch|first3=H.|year=2017|title=Behavioral responses to encounter of fishing boats in wandering albatrosses|journal=Ecology and Evolution|volume=7|issue=10|pages=3335–3347|doi=10.1002/ece3.2677|pmid=28515870 |bibcode=2017EcoEv...7.3335C |pmc=5433987}}</ref>

===Reproduction===
[[File:Grand Albatros MHNT.jpg|thumb|Egg of ''Diomedea exulans '']]
The snowy albatross mates for life and breeds every other year.<ref name="Harrison" /> At breeding time they occupy loose [[seabird colony|colonies]] on isolated island groups in the [[Southern Ocean]]. When courting they will spread their wings, wave their heads, and rap their bills together while braying.<ref name="BLI"/> Wanderers have a large range of displays from screams and whistles to grunts and bill clapping.<ref name="Robertsonft" /> They lay one egg that is white, with a few spots, and is about {{convert|10|cm|in|abbr=on}} long. They lay between 10 December and 5 January. The nests are large bowls built of grassy vegetation and soil peat,<ref name="Robertsonft" /> that is 1 meter wide at the base and half a meter wide at the apex. Incubation takes about 11 weeks and both parents are involved.<ref name="Harrison" /> The 11-week incubation period is among the longest of any bird.<ref>{{Cite web|title=What to expect on Royal Cam|url=https://www.doc.govt.nz/nature/native-animals/birds/birds-a-z/albatrosses/royal-albatross-toroa/royal-cam/what-to-expect/|access-date=14 February 2022|website=[[Department of Conservation (New Zealand)|Department of Conservation]]|language=en-nz|archive-date=29 January 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220129094020/https://www.doc.govt.nz/nature/native-animals/birds/birds-a-z/albatrosses/royal-albatross-toroa/royal-cam/what-to-expect/|url-status=live}}</ref> During the early stages of the chick's development, the parents take turns sitting on the nest while the other searches for food. Later, both adults search for food and visit the chick at irregular intervals.<ref>{{Citation |last=Tickell |first=W. L. N. |title=Antarctic Bird Studies |chapter=The Biology of the Great Albatrosses, Diomedea Exulahs and Diomedea Epomophora |date=23 March 2013 |series=Antarctic Research Series |pages=1–56 |editor-last=Austin |editor-first=Oliver L. |chapter-url=http://doi.wiley.com/10.1029/AR012p0001 |access-date=14 February 2024 |place=Washington, D. C. |publisher=American Geophysical Union |doi=10.1029/ar012p0001 |isbn=978-1-118-66914-3}}</ref> Researchers previously assumed that chicks went without food for the whole winter after a weaning period of roughly 12 to 16 weeks. Later studies disproved this concept, as chicks were found to be feeding during this period.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Berrow |first1=Simon D. |last2=Croxall |first2=John P. |date=1 May 2001 |title=Provisioning Rate and Attendance Patterns of Wandering Albatrosses at Bird Island, South Georgia |journal=The Condor |volume=103 |issue=2 |pages=230–239 |doi=10.1093/condor/103.2.230 |issn=0010-5422}}</ref> They are a [[Monogamy in animals|monogamous]] species, usually for life. Adolescents return to the colony within six years; however, they will not start breeding until 11 to 15 years.<ref name="Dunn" /> About 31.5% of [[fledge|fledglings]] survive.<ref name="Robertsonft" /> They can live for over 50 years.<ref name="PhysOrg">[http://www.physorg.com/news188587622.html Is foraging efficiency a key parameter in aging?] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110916220541/http://www.physorg.com/news188587622.html |date=16 September 2011 }}. Physorg (23 March 2010)</ref>

==Relationship with humans==

[[File:Scaling-of-Soaring-Seabirds-and-Implications-for-Flight-Abilities-of-Giant-Pterosaurs-pone.0005400.s005.ogv|thumb|Individual taking off]]
[[Sailor|Sailors]] used to capture the birds for their long wing bones, from which they made tobacco pipe stems.  The early explorers of the great Southern Sea were cheered by the companionship of the albatross in their dreary solitudes, and as shown in [[Samuel Taylor Coleridge|Coleridge]]'s ''[[The Rime of the Ancient Mariner]]'' killing them was seen as extremely bad luck. The metaphor of "an albatross around his neck" also comes from the poem and indicates an unwanted burden causing anxiety or hindrance. In the days of sail the bird often accompanied ships for days, not merely following a ship, but wheeling in wide circles around it without ever being observed to land on the water. The bird would continue its flight, apparently untired, in tempestuous as well as moderate weather.<ref name=Chisholm/>

The [[Māori people|Māori]] of New Zealand used albatrosses as a food source. They caught them using baited hooks.<ref>{{cite web | title = Matau Toroa (Albatross hook) | work = Collections Online | publisher = Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa | url = http://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/search.aspx?term=Matau%20Toroa%20(Albatross%20hook) | access-date = 17 July 2010 | archive-date = 24 October 2012 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20121024200651/http://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/search.aspx?term=Matau%20Toroa%20(Albatross%20hook) | url-status = live }}</ref> Because the wing bones of albatross are light but very strong, Māori used them to make a number of different items including ''[[kōauau]]'' (flutes),<ref>{{cite web | title = Koauau (flute) | work = Collections Online | publisher = Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa | url = http://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/objectdetails.aspx?oid=138660 | access-date = 17 July 2010 | archive-date = 24 October 2012 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20121024200734/http://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/objectdetails.aspx?oid=138660 | url-status = live }}</ref> needles, tattooing chisel blades,<ref>{{cite web | title = Uhi Ta Moko (tattooing instruments) | work = Collections Online | publisher = Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa | url = http://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/objectdetails.aspx?oid=703621 | access-date = 17 July 2010 | archive-date = 24 October 2012 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20121024200840/http://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/objectdetails.aspx?oid=703621 | url-status = live }}</ref> and barbs for [[fish hook]]s.<ref>{{cite web | title = Matau (fish hook) | work = Collections Online | publisher = Museum of New Zealand Te Papa Tongarewa | url = http://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/objectdetails.aspx?oid=197167 | access-date = 17 July 2010 | archive-date = 24 May 2010 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20100524015201/http://collections.tepapa.govt.nz/objectdetails.aspx?oid=197167 | url-status = live }}</ref>

===Conservation===
[[File:Wonder albat.jpg|thumb|right|Snowy albatross at [[South Georgia Island#South Georgia|South Georgia Island]]]]
The [[IUCN]] lists the snowy albatross as [[Vulnerable species|vulnerable]] status.<ref name="iucn status 19 November 2021" /> Adult mortality is 5% to 7.8% per year as of 2003.<ref name="Robertsonft" /> It has an occurrence range of {{convert|64700000|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}, although its breeding range is only {{convert|1900|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}.<ref name="BLI" />

The biggest threat to their survival is that they are attracted by the bait for [[longline fishing]] and get caught in the same way as the fish; however, pollution is also taking a toll, mainly from plastics and abandoned fishing hooks. The [[CCAMLR]] has introduced measures to reduce [[bycatch]] of albatrosses around South Georgia by 99%, and other regional fishing commissions are taking similar measures to reduce fatalities. The [[Prince Edward Islands]] are a nature preserve, the [[Macquarie Islands]] are a [[World Heritage]] site, and large parts of the [[Crozet Islands]] and the [[Kerguelen Islands]] are nature reserves.<ref name="BLI" />
